lab_description: We follow research on ATOMISTIC SIMULATION strictly: computational methods that represent chemical or material systems as explicit atoms (or coarse-grained beads with an atomistic mapping) and compute their energies, forces, dynamics, or thermodynamics from a potential. The application domain doesn't matter (chemistry, materials science, biophysics, soft matter, geophysics, all fine) AS LONG AS the work is genuinely about atomistic simulation, either classical or ML-based. IN SCOPE: classical molecular dynamics; ab initio / DFT molecular dynamics (Born-Oppenheimer, Car-Parrinello); path-integral MD; Monte Carlo on atomic configurations; geometry optimization, transition-state search, NEB; free-energy methods (metadynamics, umbrella sampling, replica exchange, alchemical, thermodynamic integration); coarse-grained MD; enhanced sampling and learned samplers (Boltzmann generators, normalizing-flow samplers tied to a potential); machine-learned interatomic potentials (NequIP, MACE, Allegro, SchNet, GemNet, PaiNN, MatterSim, UMA, Orb, eSEN, GNoME, ANI, AIMNet, etc.), including development, training-data strategies, transferability, benchmarking, or application; classical force-field development (ReaxFF, EAM, Stillinger-Weber); DFT method development (functionals, GW/BSE, DFT+U), post-Hartree-Fock methods (CCSD, MP2), embedding theories (QM/MM, DMFT), semi-empirical / tight-binding (DFTB, GFN-xTB); equivariant or geometric neural networks USED FOR atomistic systems; diffusion / flow-matching / score-based models that generate 3D atomic configurations AND are tied to a potential or validated energetically; crystal structure prediction / polymorph search using DFT or MLIPs. STRICTLY OUT OF SCOPE (score below 0.5 even if buzzwords match): property prediction from SMILES / 2D graphs / fingerprints with no explicit 3D atomic coordinates; LLM agents for chemistry or materials that don't actually run simulations; high-throughput screening pipelines that only use descriptors, fingerprints, or compositional features; retrosynthesis, reaction-outcome prediction, synthesis planning; mass spectrometry / fragmentation / spectrum prediction; protein structure prediction (AlphaFold-style sequence-to-structure); drug docking, virtual screening of drug-like libraries, ADMET prediction; XRD, diffraction, or other inverse problems that don't run atomistic dynamics; pure ML methodology (architectures, generic diffusion models, generic equivariant networks) with no atomistic application or energetic validation; cheminformatics on 2D representations.
